column1: Comprehensive Timeline of Scythians, Sakas, and Related Steppe Tribes
Religion and Mythology of the Scythians and Related Peoples
Cosmology
Tripartite Universe: Common Indo-European division of cosmos into upper (heavenly), middle (earthly), and lower (underworld) realms
World Tree: Central axis connecting the three realms, often depicted in art
Sacred Geography: Mountains, rivers, and certain territories held special significance
Cardinal Directions: Each associated with specific deities and powers
Pantheon
Tabiti: Supreme goddess, equivalent to Hestia/Vesta, goddess of the hearth and fire
Papaios: Sky father, equivalent to Zeus/Jupiter, chief male deity
Api: Earth goddess, consort of Papaios, associated with fertility
Oitosyros/Goitosyros: Sun god, equivalent to Apollo
Argimpasa/Artimpasa: Fertility goddess, equivalent to Aphrodite Urania, associated with sovereignty
Thagimasadas: Water god, equivalent to Poseidon
Targitaos: Mythical ancestor of Scythians, son of Zeus and a river nymph
Religious Practices
Animal Sacrifice: Horses particularly significant; also sheep, cattle, and goats
Divination: Scapulimancy (reading shoulder blades), augury, interpretation of natural phenomena
Ritual Smoke: Cannabis and other psychoactive substances used in purification rituals
Oath-Taking: Solemn oaths taken by drinking mixed blood, often in golden vessels
Scalping: Ritual taking of enemy scalps as trophies
Sacred Fires: Maintenance of sacred flames; fire worship
Burial Practices: Elaborate kurgan burials for elites; horse sacrifices; grave goods
Shamanic Elements
Enarees: Transvestite shamans/priests mentioned by Herodotus
Soul Journey: Possible belief in shamanic soul flight
Transformation: Human-animal transformation themes in art suggests shamanic beliefs
Spirit Communication: Rituals designed to communicate with ancestors and spirits
Mythical Narratives
Origin Myth: Golden implements (plow, yoke, battle-axe, cup) fall from heaven
Divine Ancestry: Royal Scythians claimed descent from Targitaos
Amazon Tales: Stories of female warriors connected to Sarmatian women fighters
Griffin Gold: Stories of one-eyed Arimaspi fighting griffins for gold
Sacred Objects
Akinakes: Sacred daggers used in rituals and as symbols of status
Cauldrons: Large bronze vessels used in ritual feasting and sacrifice
Golden Objects: Special religious significance attached to gold items
Standards: Horse-topped standards as tribal/clan symbols
Regional Variations
Pontic Scythians: More influenced by Greek religion over time
Eastern Saka: Stronger shamanic elements; mountain worship
Massagetae: Known for sun worship and horse sacrifice
Indo-Scythians: Adopted elements of Indian religions, especially Buddhism
Late Developments
Hellenization: Adoption of Greek deities and practices by some groups
Syncretic Religions: Blending of Iranian, Greek,
Comprehensive Saka-Scythian Historical Timeline

Note: Dates are approximate for earlier periods, based on archaeological and historical evidence. The terms "Cian," "Saka," and "Scythian" are sometimes used interchangeably in historical sources but refer to specific groups within the broader nomadic Iranian cultural sphere.
